title: WT1 mutation and MRD interpretation in AML
learning_objectives: Distinguish diagnostic, prognostic and clonal markers
clinical_vignette: A 46-year-old woman has AML with normal karyotype. NGS shows WT1 mutation VAF 31% and FLT3-ITD. After induction, WT1 mutation is still detectable at low level while morphology shows remission.
question: Which statement is most appropriate?
options: A. WT1 persistence may suggest residual molecular disease but must be interpreted with assay sensitivity, baseline marker suitability and clinical context.
C. WT1 persistence always proves relapse.
D. WT1 is never useful for monitoring.
correct_answer: A. WT1 persistence may suggest residual molecular disease but must be interpreted with assay sensitivity, baseline marker suitability and clinical context.
explanation: WT1 abnormalities may have monitoring value in selected cases, but molecular persistence requires careful interpretation. MRD reporting depends on validated assays, sensitivity and disease context.
frcpath_pearl: MRD interpretation is not just positive versus negative; assay and marker suitability matter.
take_home_message: Molecular findings in AML must be interpreted in diagnostic hierarchy and clinical context.
references: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours, 5th Edition
